stem: A hospital uses a rapid antibody test to screen for HIV. The test has a sensitivity of 80% and a specificity of 73%. The prevalence of HIV in the patient population is estimated to be between 3.9% and 8.2%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has HIV? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(HIV | positive test) ranges from 0.107 to 0.209 as prevalence varies from 3.9% to 8.2%. Point estimate at 6.0% prevalence: 0.159.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.27.
The confidence interval [0.107, 0.209] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (80%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
Not recognizing the strong base rate effect when prevalence is low
